data.title: Former Canadian Olympian Ryan Wedding Arrested By The FBI For Running Transnational Crypto-Linked Drug Ring And Attempted Murder
data.description:
From Olympic Athlete to International Fugitive

U.S. Authorities have arrested former Canadian Olympic snowboarder Ryan Wedding, bringing an end to a decade-long international manhunt and marking a turn of events of how an once elite athlete could turn into the mastermind of a large scale transnational crime syndicate.

🚨 Breaking FBI most wanted: Former Olympic Snowboarder Ryan Wedding's $1B Cocaine Empire Crumbles After Reckless Social Media Slip

From Salt Lake City slopes to Sinaloa shadows. Wedding evaded the FBI and deadlier ex-associates. But an Instagram post flaunting a… pic.twitter.com/Esrrd3AqVz

— Moon (@MoonCall) January 23, 2026

U.S prosecutors has allege that Wedding evolved from an Olympic competitor into a senior figure within a cartel-linked cocaine trafficking network that relied on cryptocurrency to move and conceal illicit proceeds.

Wedding, 44, was taken into custody in Mexico late Thursday before being transferred to the United States on Friday, according to federal officials. His arrest follows years on the FBI’s Ten Most Wanted list and a reward of up to $15 million for information leading to his capture.

Wedding represented Team Canada at the 2002 Winter Olympics in Salt Lake City, a credential that investigators say later afforded him international mobility and a degree of legitimacy uncommon among traditional organized crime figures.

U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi said Wedding will face federal charges including drug trafficking, murder, and operating a criminal enterprise spanning multiple countries, describing him as a former Olympian who later became embedded in violent narcotics operations.

FBI Director Kash Patel confirmed the arrest, crediting sustained cooperation with Mexican authorities after more than ten years of efforts to locate Wedding. Officials believe he had been living in Mexico under cartel protection for much of that time while continuing to direct elements of the trafficking network.

Cartel Operations and Crypto-Based Money Flows

Investigators allege Wedding played a senior role in cocaine distribution networks tied to Mexico’s Sinaloa Cartel, overseeing shipments from Colombia into the United States and Canada. At its peak, U.S. officials say the operation generated more than $1 billion annually in illegal revenue.

In November, the U.S. Treasury Department’s Office of Foreign Assets Control sanctioned Wedding, accusing his organization of using cryptocurrency to launder drug profits.

The Ryan Wedding arrest and the timeline of events is wild. From the olympics to OFAC sanctions, top ten list at the @FBI , and now the arrest announced by @FBIDirectorKash .

Sanctioned crypto addresses across multiple blockchains (tron, solana, bitcoin, bnb, and eth) likely… pic.twitter.com/UYivvX9Tmv

— Joe McGill (@joes_mcgill) January 24, 2026

Treasury officials said digital assets were employed to obscure transaction trails and conceal large sums derived from narcotics trafficking, reflecting a broader trend of organized crime groups integrating blockchain-based financial tools into their operations.

Mexico’s Security Secretary Omar García Harfuch said Wedding voluntarily surrendered at the U.S. Embassy before being handed over to the FBI. Wedding was flown to Ontario International Airport in Southern California, where federal authorities announced the seizure of firearms, luxury vehicles, artwork, and other assets linked to the alleged criminal enterprise. Officials indicated the investigation remains active and that additional arrests are possible.

Prior Conviction and What Comes Next

Wedding’s arrest is not his first encounter with U.S. law enforcement. In 2008, he was detained in California in a cocaine trafficking case connected to a Vancouver-based operation. He was convicted the following year and sentenced to four years in prison, before being released around 2011.

Federal prosecutors are now expected to pursue a broader set of charges tied to transnational drug trafficking, financial crimes, and violent offenses. The case unfolds as regulators and law enforcement agencies continue to warn about the growing role of cryptocurrency in large-scale criminal networks.

As part of the Giant Slalom investigative developments announced last week involving FBI Top Ten Fugitive, Ryan Wedding, and his associates, a rare 2002 Mercedes CLK-GTR valued at $13 million was seized by the FBI.
Wedding wanted poster: https://t.co/PCwWjFU578pic.twitter.com/aBBfVbbUfS

— FBI Los Angeles (@FBILosAngeles) November 25, 2025

Blockchain analytics firm Chainalysis reported that illicit cryptocurrency addresses received an estimated $154 billion in 2025, underscoring the scale at which digital assets are now intersecting with global crime.

Supplementary disclosures from U.S. Treasury officials and court filings further detail the alleged financial architecture behind Wedding’s organization. Authorities say the network relied not only on cryptocurrency broadly, but on a coordinated, multi-chain laundering strategy spanning Bitcoin, Ethereum, Tron, Solana, and BNB Chain.

Treasury sanctions issued in November linked Wedding and his associates to a web of crypto wallets and front entities designed to move and conceal proceeds at scale.

Prosecutors have pointed to the use of stablecoins as a transactional layer within the operation, citing documented USDT transfers tied to cocaine transactions. While officials have not released the full scope of onchain activity, they described the structure as consistent with an enterprise capable of supporting hundreds of kilograms of cocaine shipments across borders.

Violence Allegations and Ongoing Prosecution

Beyond narcotics trafficking, federal prosecutors allege Wedding directed acts of violence connected to the organization’s operations. Court records accuse him of ordering the killing of a cooperating federal witness in Colombia in January 2025, as well as authorizing retaliatory shootings in Canada linked to stolen drug shipments.

A superseding indictment unsealed in June 2024 charged Wedding with operating a continuing criminal enterprise, major drug trafficking offenses, and murder in connection with those activities. Wedding was taken into custody in Mexico City and transferred to the United States following coordination between U.S. and Mexican authorities.

Officials said he is expected to face federal court proceedings in the coming weeks, with prosecutors signaling that the case remains active and that additional charges or arrests tied to the alleged network are possible.

data.title: Is Solana's Seeker mobile token SKR a viable model?
data.description:
Source: Galaxy; Compiled by: Jinse Finance

Solana Labs launched the Seeker token (SKR) last week, linked to its second-generation mobile device, Seeker, marking the latest milestone in the Solana ecosystem's years-long foray into consumer hardware.

Unlike traditional smartphones, Seeker was designed from the outset as a crypto-native application. It integrates on-device key management, OS-level secure transaction signing, native wallet and payment support through a secure zone called Seed Vault, and provides direct access to a Solana app store that bypasses Apple and Google's distribution and fee structures.

Seeker follows the Saga phone. Released in 2023, the Saga phone initially received a lukewarm reception but gained demand later in the Memecoin boom. At the time, airdrops from the Solana ecosystem made buying the Saga phone a profitable deal. Approximately 20,000 Saga phones were eventually sold before Solana Mobile (Labs' hardware subsidiary) discontinued support for the Saga.

Seeker phones have shown strong early market performance, with over 150,000 devices activated in more than 50 countries. SKR is at the heart of the platform's design. As the white paper states, this token is used for staking and electing "Guardians," who are responsible for verifying device authenticity, auditing applications in the Solara and App Store, and enforcing platform rules. Developers submit applications by staking SKR, thus building a mechanism to resist Sybil attacks and punish malicious behavior; users and stakers delegate SKR to Guardians in exchange for inflationary rewards. The platform intentionally delays monetization; fee collection and discounts may be introduced after the ecosystem matures. Our View: Despite significant progress in blockchain space and infrastructure, distribution and adoption remain ongoing challenges for cryptocurrencies. Launching applications is no longer the challenge (as is issuing tokens, thanks to Pump.fun and similar platforms). The real challenge remains ensuring these applications consistently and repeatedly reach users, avoiding speculative cycles. Most cryptocurrency adoption has been explosive growth driven by airdrops, hype, or short-lived crazes, quickly fading away. What's lacking are channels that support sustained user engagement rather than one-off interactions. Mobile devices offer a way to change this. This is not only because they expand reach, but also because they integrate cryptocurrency into people's daily lives, such as payments, signatures and authentication, social applications, and games. The native crypto mobile technology stack has the potential to make on-chain activity as natural as everyday life. This is similar to the early development of mobile devices, where basic technologies like GPS, cameras, and security hardware spawned new applications. Native crypto mobile hardware introduces its own unique foundational technologies—biometric signatures, device verification, and native payments—technologies difficult to replicate on desktop and potentially spawning applications that don't yet exist. The real challenge lies in getting new users to get these phones in the first place. Indeed, the 150,000 phones sold since Saga's launch are impressive. But it's unclear how many of these are new cryptocurrency users, and they represent only a small fraction of Solana's active wallets. Part of Solana's bet is that if they can offer an affordable, fully-featured phone, even non-crypto users will be interested. Airdrops are the icing on the cake. Solana Mobile's ambitions extend far beyond Solana-branded hardware. If the model proves viable, the platform aims to expand to other devices and manufacturers. Seeker is best viewed as a testing ground to see if crypto-native user experiences, incentive mechanisms, and governance methods can support a sustainable mobile ecosystem. The risks are obvious. The hardware industry is fiercely competitive, incentives can easily lead to short-sighted behavior, and revenue generation is not instantaneous. But this trade-off is well-considered. Solana prioritizes distribution and user engagement over short-term profits. Even if only a small fraction of on-chain activity begins through the mobile interface, it could become a much-needed new way to onboard users. Solana founder Anatoly Yakovenko, who worked in the mobile space before founding Solana, has a similar definition of success. Their goal is not to completely replace Apple or Google. The real victory lies in forcing existing platforms to lower fees, relax restrictions, or improve support for crypto-native experiences. If building a trustworthy alternative can influence platform behavior, that alone will contribute to the development of the entire ecosystem.

data.title: "Prediction" prediction market
data.description:
Author:Nishil Jain,Translator:：Block unicorn

Foreword

I often visit the Polymarket website to check the latest developments in global hot news. For example, how likely is it that the United States will occupy Greenland? Or, what will the final pricing (FDV) of Monad be at the time of release?

Vitalik also uses the market as a news tool, and perhaps many other analysts and people around the world have already made this shift.

Vitalik also uses the market as a news tool, and perhaps many other analysts and people around the world have now made this shift.

Soon, we'll see these markets integrated into our existing news feeds and social apps. In fact, we haven't heard any updates on X's partnership with Polymarket since last year. Just last October, as part of its $2 billion strategic investment, Intercontinental Exchange (ICE, the parent company of the New York Stock Exchange) announced plans to explore commercial applications of Polymarket data. While these feed integrations are still in progress, prediction markets have already successfully integrated into financial apps like Coinbase and Robinhood. Kalshi is entering the web3 app market through the Jupiter super app and Phantom Wallet, while Polymarket has partnered with MetaMask and Rabby Wallet. Prediction market trading volume has remained strong even after the election peak. Currently, daily trading volume on prediction markets exceeds $800 million—more than Pumpfun's $775 million as of this writing.

But today, we're not going to discuss the obvious phenomenon of the rise of prediction markets (which is now a widely accepted fact). We'll explore the trends to come.

The rise of pump.fun spawned an ecosystem of Telegram bots, trading terminals, and DeFi ecosystems that power these tokens. Similarly, we're now seeing the rise of applications that expand the concept of prediction markets beyond its existing forms—for example, lending and borrowing prediction market positions, leveraged trading of your predictions, opinion markets, decision markets, and so on.

... I know this all sounds strange, but after reading this article, you'll understand everything. Galaxy Research recently published an article titled "The Future Landscape of Prediction Markets," outlining emerging mechanisms that go beyond basic event prediction and its technical details. This article draws on the concepts explored in that article and adds some personal additions. Overall, next-generation prediction market applications are evolving in three main directions: Expanding into decentralized finance (DeFi) through derivatives and lending businesses; applying artificial intelligence to prediction outcomes and making it the default interface for interacting with those outcomes; and the evolution of prediction markets themselves, moving beyond simple event prediction to include decision-making markets, opinion-based markets, and markets where predictions impact asset prices.

DeFi Expansion

When a platform gathers people with different risk appetites and trading habits, it becomes very challenging to meet everyone's needs on a single platform. For example, users who want to exchange ETH for USDT cannot do so on Uniswap. This is where Aave or Hyperliquid come in handy.

Gondor is building a lending platform for prediction market traders. After depositing funds, traders can borrow up to 50% USDC as collateral with their holdings. The borrowed funds will be directly returned to Polymarket and displayed as the trader's "cash balance".

Through Gondor, traders can free up previously idle funds, reinvesting them in new trades while maintaining investment exposure.

Of course, all of this follows certain rules. The Gondor team manually screens specific market positions that meet the collateral requirements. These rules ensure that illiquid and easily manipulated markets are excluded from the platform. Many factors determine which markets are whitelisted: order book depth, clarity of solution standards, and solution completion time. The team recently raised $2.5 million in seed funding, with participation from Castle Island Ventures, Maven 11, and Prelude. Gondor's purpose is to bet that predicted market share will become a standardized collateral asset class. Then there's Space, which allows users to gain leveraged exposure to event outcomes. Suppose there's a market for "Will Monad FDV's market capitalization exceed $8 billion when it goes public?", where the price for "yes" is $0.15 (meaning a 15% probability). A trader buys 1000 "yes" shares—normally this would require $150, but with 5x leverage, only $30 is needed as margin. If the probability rises to 30%, the position will be worth $300, and the $30 margin will yield a 500% return on equity ($150 profit). If the probability drops to 13.33% ($0.13), the trader will be forced to liquidate and lose the $30 margin. Space allows traders to profit even from small changes in probability. For example, someone anticipating an overall rise in the cryptocurrency market could bet on the Monad FDV market and profit from a slight increase in its probability. Furthermore, many cryptocurrency users from the memecoin ecosystem, AI coin projects, and more trade with the goal of turning $10 into $1000. For them, and for users with extensive market knowledge but limited funds, these markets can be an attractive investment channel.

AI and Prediction Markets

Vitalik, in his blog "Info Finance," offers some insights into the application of artificial intelligence in prediction markets.

One ​​of his points is to leverage AI to achieve high-quality participation in all micro-markets, which are otherwise too small to attract skilled human traders.

Many of the most interesting prediction markets help us obtain information; they all target "micro" problems: millions of micro-markets, each with relatively small consequences for decisions.

Importantly, the motivation for operating these agencies is not solely based on trading profits. In many cases, the agent's funding may come from organizations or individuals who prioritize market information output; trading activity here serves merely as a signal aggregation mechanism rather than an independent source of revenue. In these micro-markets, human attention is scarce, while AI's attention is abundant. Here, prediction markets no longer resemble gambling products but rather information engines. AI reduces participation costs to near zero, allowing the market to generate meaningful signals even with small trading volumes. Imagine thousands of small markets operating in parallel, each efficiently priced by AI agents. A second use of AI is as the default interface layer for prediction markets. As the number of markets increases, tracking them across multiple platforms becomes a significant cognitive burden for any trader. AI can translate a user's natural language input into tradable positions. For example, a user could tell AI that they believe silver prices will break $100 per ounce by the end of February—AI can then identify potential trading opportunities where the user can profit from this insight—whether in silver options or silver price prediction markets.

Evolutionary Prediction Market Design

Impact Markets

There's a strange gap in how markets work today: you can bet on whether something will happen, and you can see the current price of an asset, but it's difficult to know what the market believes that asset will be worth if a particular event occurs.

Prediction markets show a 60% chance that Trump will raise tariffs on China. The spot market might show Bitcoin trading at $100,000, but you can't know what the market generally believes Bitcoin will trade if the tariffs are actually implemented.

Impact Markets solve this problem. Instead of making "yes/no" bets on events through synthetic tokens, it trades real assets with conditional states.

Impact Markets solve this problem.

You're essentially saying, "I'd only be willing to buy Bitcoin for $90,000 (10% below the current market price) if tariffs are imposed." This applies to any asset-event combination. "If Robinhood launches cryptocurrency trading, use COIN as the underlying asset," "If asteroid mining becomes a reality by 2030, use gold as the underlying asset." You get my point. Events and their impacts are fundamentally different. Prediction markets answer "Will this happen?", while impact markets answer "If this happens, what will happen to this asset?" Decision Markets go a step further, from information transmission to action. They not only reveal the market's thoughts but also allow the market to determine the organization's actual actions. This functionality has already been achieved through Futarchy DAOs on platforms like MetaDAO. Here's how it works: An organization proposes a decision, such as firing its Chief Technology Officer (CTO). The market trades in two conditional states: Pass and Fail. Each state prices the organization's token differently. If the token price rises in the Pass state, the proposal is executed; if the token price rises in the Fail state, the proposal is rejected. The market decides which action maximizes expected value. Transactions are settled only based on the winning outcome. Opinion Markets Not all important questions have clear answers. Prediction markets require objective results—"Did X happen?" These results are easily verifiable. But many economically relevant questions are not. Which prediction market is currently receiving the most attention? Has market sentiment become more optimistic after Powell's last speech? These questions influence capital allocation but cannot be answered with simple "yes" or "no." This is where opinion markets come into play. Platforms like Noise.xyz allow you to speculate on various narratives themselves. These narratives are not determined by deterministic predictions or binary rules but function as a continuous emotional tool. Prices reflect the market's collective opinion at any given moment, not the eventual "correct" outcome. If the price of your bet goes up, you make money; if it goes down, you lose money. It's that simple. Traditional prediction markets are constrained by adjudication requirements: the questions must be objectively verifiable, unambiguous, and answerable with credible data. Opinion markets, however, trade emotions, which tend to be constantly changing. Points to Consider: Prediction markets are still in their early stages, and most markets have low liquidity. A few thousand dollars can cause share prices to fluctuate by more than 10%. In this situation, the probability displayed on the screen is less a "market consensus" and more a "view of the last person to invest $5,000." Only the most liquid markets, such as presidential elections or major sporting events, can provide reliable price signals. Everything else is easily manipulated. People with enough capital and strong conviction can artificially create consensus. Once involved, they can achieve this through persistent price fluctuations in obscure markets. Oracles are another potential point of failure. Blockchains cannot perceive the workings of the real world. They require external infrastructure to input information. Who controls this infrastructure? How do they make decisions? What happens when the flow of funds depends on their judgment? This question arose in Polymarket's trading regarding "Zelensky wearing a suit." Despite multiple news outlets reporting that he was wearing a suit, market authorities ruled that he wasn't, sparking intense controversy in the market. The crux of the controversy lies in the style of his suit, a point for which there is clearly no definitive answer. Those who run oracles are driven by economic interests. Sometimes these interests motivate them to provide accurate information. Sometimes they don't. Or most of the time, the information itself may be ambiguous. As these markets develop and become mainstream, we will see change, and platforms will mature to address these issues. Let's see how things go in a few months.

data.title: a16z: What to do when market predictions fail?
data.description:
Author: Andy Hall, Research Advisor at a16z and Professor of Political Economy at Stanford University; Source: a16z crypto; Compiled by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Last year, the market for predicting the Venezuelan presidential election results saw trading exceeding $6 million. However, after the vote count, the market faced a dilemma: the government declared Nicolás Maduro the winner; the opposition and international observers claimed election fraud. Should the settlement of prediction market contracts be based on "official information" (Maduro's victory) or "the consensus of credible reporting" (the opposition's victory)?

In the Venezuelan elections, observers raised a variety of accusations: rules were ignored, participants' money was "stolen"; the dispute resolution mechanism acted as "judge, jury, and executioner" in a high-stakes political farce; and the election process itself was "severely manipulated." This is not an isolated incident, but rather one of the biggest bottlenecks in the scaling up of predictive markets: the manifestation of contract settlement. The risks are high. If the outcome is accurately predicted, people will trust your market and be willing to trade in it, and prices will become a meaningful signal to society. If the outcome is mispredicted, trading becomes frustrating and unpredictable. Participants may leave, liquidity risks drying up, and prices no longer reflect accurate predictions of stable objectives. Instead, prices begin to reflect a vague mixture between the actual probability of the outcome and traders' beliefs about how the distorted outcome-determining mechanism will rule. While the Venezuelan dispute has garnered significant attention, similar errors have occurred on various platforms: The Ukraine map manipulation incident demonstrates that adversaries can directly manipulate dispute resolution mechanisms. A contract regarding territorial control stipulated that the outcome would be determined based on a specific online map. It is alleged that someone tampered with the map to influence the contract's outcome. When your factual basis can be altered, your market can be manipulated. Government shutdown trading contracts demonstrate that settlement criteria can be inaccurate, even leading to unpredictable results. Settlement rules stipulated that the market would settle based on the government shutdown end date displayed on the Office of Personnel Management (OPM) website. President Trump signed the appropriations bill on November 12th, but for unknown reasons, the OPM website was not updated until November 13th. Traders who correctly predicted the shutdown would end on the 12th lost their bets due to the website administrator's delayed update. The market for Zelensky wearing a suit sparked concerns about conflicts of interest. The contract asked Ukrainian President Zelensky whether he would wear a suit on a particular occasion—a seemingly insignificant question—yet attracted over $200 million in bets. When Zelensky appeared at the NATO summit in the suit described by the BBC, the New York Post, and other media outlets, the market initially answered "yes." However, Universal Market Access (UMA Protocol) token holders disputed the result, ultimately changing it to "no." In this article, I explore how the clever combination of Large Language Models (LLMs) and cryptography can help us create large-scale prediction market solutions that are difficult to manipulate, while maintaining accuracy, complete transparency, and credible neutrality. This isn't just a prediction market problem. Similar dilemmas plague financial markets as well. The International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) has been struggling for years to address the liquidation challenges of the credit default swap market (a type of contract that pays compensation in the event of a company's or country's debt default), and its 2024 assessment report candidly pointed out these difficulties. ISDA's determination committee, composed of major market participants, votes on whether a credit event has occurred. However, like the UMA's process, it is criticized for a lack of transparency, potential conflicts of interest, and inconsistent results. The fundamental problem remains the same: when large sums of money depend on determining whether an ambiguous situation has occurred, every resolution mechanism becomes a target for exploitation, and every ambiguity can become a potential trigger. So, what should a good resolution mechanism look like? Characteristics of a Good Solution Any viable solution needs to achieve several key characteristics simultaneously. **Capability to Resist Manipulation:** If adversaries can influence outcomes by editing Wikipedia, spreading fake news, bribing authorities, or exploiting loopholes, the market becomes a game of manipulation, not prediction. **Reasonable Accuracy:** The mechanism must make correct judgments in most cases. Absolute accuracy is impossible in a world of genuine ambiguity, but systematic errors or obvious mistakes severely damage credibility. **Prior Transparency is Crucial:** Traders need a complete understanding of how the settlement mechanism works before placing bets. Changing rules during trading violates the fundamental agreement between the platform and participants. **Credible Neutrality:** Participants need to trust that the mechanism does not favor any particular trader or outcome. This is why it's so problematic to let participants holding large numbers of UMA contracts resolve the contracts they've bet on: even if they act fairly, the appearance of a conflict of interest undermines trust. Human committees can satisfy some of these characteristics, but fall short in others—especially at large scales, struggling to effectively resist manipulation and maintain credible neutrality. Token-based voting systems like UMA also suffer from well-known whale dominance and conflicts of interest. This is where artificial intelligence (AI) comes in. The rationale for using Large Language Models (LLMs) for judgment: In the prediction market space, a proposal is gaining traction: using Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges, locking specific models and hints into the blockchain when contracts are created. The basic architecture works as follows: When a contract is created, the market maker specifies not only the resolution criteria in natural language, but also the exact LLM (identified by a timestamped model version) and the precise hints used to determine the outcome. This specification is then cryptographically written to the blockchain. At the start of a transaction, participants can view the complete settlement mechanism—they know exactly which AI model will determine the outcome, what hints it will receive, and which information sources it can access. If they don't like this trading method, they won't trade. During settlement, the committed LLM runs according to the committed hints, accesses all specified information sources, and makes a judgment. This judgment determines who will receive compensation. This approach addresses several key constraints simultaneously: LLMs are highly resistant to manipulation (though not absolutely). Unlike Wikipedia pages or small news websites, the output of an LLM model cannot be easily edited. The model's weights are determined at generation time. To manipulate the results, an adversary would either need to corrupt the information sources the model relies on or contaminate the model's training data in some way well in advance—both of which are costly and extremely risky compared to bribing oracles or tampering with maps. High accuracy. With the rapid development of inference models, capable of handling a wide variety of complex tasks, especially those that can browse the web and search for new information, LLM models should be able to accurately solve many market problems—experiments to understand their accuracy are still ongoing. Complete transparency. The entire settlement mechanism is open, transparent, and auditable before anyone places a bet. There are no rule changes during the competition, no arbitrary judgments, and no behind-the-scenes deals. You are fully aware of what you are participating in. This significantly enhances credibility and neutrality. The LLM model has no economic interest in the outcome, is not bribed, and does not hold UMA tokens. Its biases, whatever they may be, stem from the model itself, not from ad-hoc decisions by stakeholders. Of course, the LLM model's judgments also have some limitations, which I will outline and discuss below: Models can also make mistakes. For example, LLM might misinterpret news reports, fabricate facts, or apply judgment criteria inconsistently. However, as long as traders understand which model they are using, they can account for its shortcomings. If a model has a particular bias in handling ambiguous cases, experienced traders will adjust it. The model doesn't need to be perfect, but it must be predictable. Manipulation isn't impossible, it's just much more difficult. If the prompt specifies a particular news source, an attacker could attempt to insert reports into those sources. This attack is costly for large media organizations but could be effective for smaller ones—essentially another form of map-tampering. The design of the prompt is crucial: a parsing mechanism that relies on diverse, redundant information sources is more robust than one that relies on a single point of failure. In theory, poisoning attacks are possible. An adversary with sufficient resources could attempt to influence the training data of an LLM model, thereby affecting its future judgments. But this requires action long before the contract is created, with uncertain returns and high costs—far more difficult than bribing committee members. The proliferation of LLM models can cause coordination problems. Liquidity becomes fragmented if different market participants choose different LLMs and trading tips. Traders struggle to compare contracts or aggregate information across markets. Standardization is important, but allowing the market to discover which LLM and trading tip combinations work best is equally crucial. The right approach might be a balance: allow experimentation, but also establish mechanisms that allow the market to gradually and consistently adopt well-tested default settings over time. How can developers adopt these strategies? In short, AI-based solutions essentially replace previous problems (human bias, conflicts of interest, information asymmetry) with a different set of problems (model limitations, engineering challenges, information source vulnerabilities), which may be easier to solve. So how do we move forward? Platforms should: Establish a track record by testing LLM mechanisms on low-risk contracts. Which models perform best? Which hint structures are most robust? What failure modes occur in practice? Standardization. As best practices emerge, the industry should work towards developing standardized LLM-hint combinations as the default option. This is not to hinder innovation, but rather to help concentrate liquidity in easily understood markets. Build transparency tools, such as interfaces that allow traders to view the full settlement mechanism (including models, hints, and information sources) before trading. Settlement mechanisms should not be hidden in inconspicuous details. Continuous governance. Even with AI judgment, humans still need to make meta-level decisions: which models to trust, how to handle situations where models give clearly incorrect answers, and when to update default settings. The goal is not to completely exclude humans, but to guide them from ad-hoc, case-by-case judgments to systemic rule-making. Prediction markets possess immense potential to help us understand the complexities of the world. However, this potential depends on trust, which in turn depends on fair contract resolution mechanisms. We have already witnessed the consequences of failed resolution mechanisms: chaos, anger, and the exodus of traders. I have personally witnessed people completely abandon prediction markets after feeling cheated because their betting results seemed to contradict their initial intentions—they vowed never to use their previously favored platforms again. This undoubtedly misses a golden opportunity to unleash the potential of prediction markets and expand their wider applications. LLM models are not perfect. But when combined with cryptography, they can achieve transparency, neutrality, and resistance to the manipulation that plagues human-based systems. This may be exactly what we need now that prediction markets are expanding far faster than governance mechanisms.

data.title: Markets and Reality: Potential Cracks Beneath the Superficial Prosperity of the US Economy in 2026
data.description:
Introduction

At the beginning of 2026, US economic data showed strong momentum. The GDP growth rate for the third quarter of 2025 was revised upward to 4.4%, accelerating from 3.8% in the second quarter. This figure reflects the positive contributions of consumer spending, exports, government spending, and investment, while reduced imports further supported growth. According to the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model, the GDP growth rate for the fourth quarter of 2025 is projected to be 5.4%. On the surface, the stock market is all in the green, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq indices both hitting new highs, the unemployment rate stabilizing at around 4.4%, and the core PCE inflation rate remaining near 2.7%. However, beneath these positive indicators lie multiple structural problems, including bond market volatility, debt refinancing pressures, asset bubble risks, and geopolitical uncertainty.

Stock Market: Bubble Alarm and Market Capitalization/GDP Ratio

The US stock market had a strong start to 2026, with both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq reaching record highs. However, the market capitalization/GDP ratio (the Buffett Indicator) reached 221.7%, far exceeding the historical average of 80%-90%. This level is close to the peak of the 2000 tech bubble, suggesting severe overvaluation. Total market capitalization reached $68.95 trillion, while annualized GDP was $31.10 trillion, reflecting a disconnect between stock market growth and the real economy.

Stock Market: Bubble Alarm and Market Capitalization/GDP Ratio

The US stock market had a strong start to 2026, with both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq reaching record highs. However, the market capitalization/GDP ratio reached 221.7%, far exceeding the historical average of 80%-90%. This level is close to the peak of the 2000 tech bubble, suggesting severe overvaluation. Total market capitalization reached $68.95 trillion, while annualized GDP was $31.10 trillion, reflecting a disconnect between stock market growth and the real economy.

Analysis shows that the triple bubble (credit, real estate, and stocks) reinforces each other. The credit bubble has already shown signs of cracking, with short positions in long-term government bonds increasing. If yields break through 5%, corporate bond issuance and mortgage rates will surge, triggering a chain reaction. Goldman Sachs predicts a total return of 12% for the S&P 500 in 2026, based on 12% earnings per share growth. However, if geopolitical risks or inflation rebound, the market could correct by 20%-30%. J.P. Morgan warns that AI-driven earnings growth may slow, amplifying valuation risks. Investors should be wary of further expansion in the market capitalization/GDP ratio, which could trigger a "bear market" correction. Debt Refinancing: The Test of $9 Trillion in Mature Debts

Debt Refinancing: The Test of $9 Trillion in Mature Debts

data.title: Japanese company plans to launch crypto ETF; South Korean prosecutors suspect seized BTC has been lost.
data.description:
Headline

▌Nomura Holdings, SBI Holdings, and other financial groups plan to launch Japan's first cryptocurrency exchange-traded funds (ETFs), with approval expected as early as 2028.

▌Gwangju Prosecutors' Office in South Korea suspected of losing hundreds of billions of won worth of seized Bitcoin, currently conducting an internal investigation

The Gwangju District Prosecutors' Office in South Korea has recently been suspected of losing a large amount of Bitcoin seized and stored in connection with a criminal case. The exact amount of Bitcoin lost has not been announced, but it is understood to be worth hundreds of billions of won. The prosecution is conducting an internal investigation.

The Gwangju District Prosecutors' Office discovered the loss during a routine inspection of seized financial assets for irregularities. They were examining Bitcoin-related passwords stored on a USB flash drive. A prosecutor stated, "We understand that the incident occurred during a routine inspection of seized Bitcoins, when the perpetrator inadvertently visited a so-called 'fake (fraudulent) website.' Internal discussions within the prosecution suggest the lost Bitcoins are worth as much as 70 billion won." A Gwangju District Prosecutors' Office official, responding to media inquiries, stated, "We cannot confirm this." As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $86,257.71, a 24-hour change of -3.3%; ETH price is $2,797.55, a 24-hour change of -5.1%; BNB price is $861.96, a 24-hour change of -2.7%. The price of SOL is $118.76, a 24-hour change of -6.6%; the price of DOGE is $0.1189, a 24-hour change of -4.3%; the price of XRP is $1.83, a 24-hour change of -4.6%; and the price of TRX is $0.295, a 24-hour change of +0.1%. WLFI price is $0.1639, a 24-hour change of -8.3%; HYPE price is $22.04, a 24-hour change of -5.4%. Policy: US Crypto Asset Regulation Accelerates: Structural Bill Promotes Trading Transparency and May Benefit Retail Investors. US cryptocurrency regulation is accelerating. If the proposed crypto market structure bill is ultimately passed, it will clarify the regulatory authority of federal regulators over digital assets, making cryptocurrencies easier to manage, track, and trade, potentially attracting more investors and increasing token value. It is reported that crypto platforms such as Coinbase and Kraken are expected to follow a registration system, while stablecoin issuers such as Circle and Tether will need to meet bank-like regulatory requirements to ensure the safety of retail investors' assets. The subsequent process includes: approval by two Senate committees, a full Senate vote, final signature by the House of Representatives, and finally, signature by Trump. Overall, most crypto investors will not be affected in the short term, but in the long run, the bill is expected to provide a safer and more predictable trading environment, while making the compliance operations of crypto platforms more transparent. Polymarket predicts a 77% probability of a US government shutdown before the end of January. Polymarket data shows that traders predict a 77% chance of another US government shutdown before the end of January, a probability that rose 67% in the past 24 hours. This comes after US President Donald Trump stated that he might face another government shutdown caused by Democrats. This expectation adds uncertainty to the CLARITY Act legislative timeline, after Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong and other executives withdrew their support for the current version of the bill. Galaxy Digital's research director, Alex Thorn, stated that negotiations regarding stablecoin yields remain deadlocked, with no significant consensus reached on a compromise. Scroll announced on the X platform that its co-founder's X account, @shenhaichen, has been compromised and they are actively working to recover it. Scroll advises against interacting with any links or private messages. Cryptocurrency ▌a16z Crypto: The Threat of Quantum Attacks is Exaggerated; Security Focus for Public Chains like BTC and ETH Remains on Protocols and Governance a16z Crypto's official Twitter account published a lengthy article titled "Quantum computing and blockchains: Matching urgency to actual threats," which points out that the timeline for the emergence of quantum computers (CRQC) capable of cracking cryptocurrencies is often exaggerated, and the likelihood of them appearing before 2030 is extremely low. For example, digital signatures and zkSNARKs are not easily affected by "HNDL" attacks, and hastily implementing blockchain migrations could bring a series of risks such as performance costs, immature implementation, and code vulnerabilities. For most public blockchains (such as Bitcoin and Ethereum), the more pressing challenges are not quantum computing, but rather protocol upgrade coordination, governance complexity, and security vulnerabilities at the implementation level. It is recommended to plan ahead rather than rush to switch to quantum-resistant solutions, based on a rational assessment of the time window. a16z Crypto emphasizes that security issues such as code vulnerabilities, side-channel attacks, and fault injection attacks are more urgent than quantum risks in the coming years, and developers should prioritize investing in code auditing, fuzzing, and formal verification. Michael Saylor, co-founder of Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) and head of the Bitcoin Reserve, stated that the biggest threat to the Bitcoin network is "ambitious opportunists" who are trying to push for changes to the Bitcoin protocol. Saylor's remarks have sparked heated debate online. Bitcoin extremist Justin Bechler believes this statement primarily targets software developers who promote non-monetary uses of Bitcoin, such as introducing NFTs on the Bitcoin chain or writing images directly into blocks. Investor Fred Krueger stated, "The biggest risk facing Bitcoin is quantum computing." Meanwhile, Mert Mumtaz, CEO of RPC node service provider Helius, explicitly disagreed with Saylor's view, stating, "This mentality is cancerous. 'Ambition and a desire to drive technological evolution are the biggest risks.' No system is absolutely error-free, and that includes Bitcoin. Like all software, Bitcoin has had numerous vulnerabilities to date—perhaps we should fix these vulnerabilities instead of letting them persist." Several users (including Mark of Bitcoin) also mentioned the ongoing spam transaction controversy and Bitcoin Improvement Proposal BIP-110—a temporary soft fork designed to filter non-monetary data in the Bitcoin ledger. Saylor's statement further exacerbated the divisions within the Bitcoin community: one faction advocated "ossify" the Bitcoin protocol and minimizing changes; the other faction, comprised of software developers and technology supporters, advocated for introducing more features to Bitcoin, such as quantum-resistant wallet addresses and on-chain file storage.

▌Michael Saylor Posts Bitcoin Tracker Information Again

Strategy founder Michael Saylor posted information related to Bitcoin Tracker again, writing "Unstoppable Orange." Based on past patterns, Strategy always discloses information about increasing its Bitcoin holdings the day after such news is released.

▌Bitcoin Exchange Internal Traffic Drops to a New Low Since 2022

According to data disclosed by CryptoOnchain, the total internal traffic of Bitcoin exchanges has dropped to its lowest level since 2022, approximately 14,000 BTC.

... This metric tracks BTC flow within exchanges, reflecting operational activity and short-term distribution readiness. Its continued decline indicates a significant reduction in BTC flowing within exchanges, pointing to weakened market-making activity and tightening liquidity. On Binance, its internal flow hovers near an all-time low of approximately 2700 BTC. Lower internal flow typically aligns with increased holding activity, reduced arbitrage activity, thinner order books, and increased sensitivity to shocks. The Bitcoin market currently appears to be in a "liquidity pause" phase, historically a precursor to sharp directional changes after market activity resumes. The pace of Bitcoin whale accumulation is encouraging; wallets holding at least 1000 BTC have accumulated 104,340 BTC (a 1.5% increase), bringing their total holdings to 7.17 million BTC, a four-month high. In addition, daily transfers exceeding $1 million have rebounded to their highest level in two months. According to BitcoinTreasuries.NET, among the top 100 publicly traded companies holding the most Bitcoin (as of January 25, 2026), four companies increased their Bitcoin holdings and one company reduced its holdings in the past seven days. The top 100 publicly traded companies collectively hold 1,127,981 Bitcoins. The VanEck Avalanche Spot ETF (VAVX) will officially begin trading on Nasdaq on Monday. Flow Traders has been designated as the primary market maker for the ETF. The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) stated that any political or personal financial relationships will not influence the procedural review of WLFI's application for a banking license. Anti-crypto Senator Elizabeth Warren requested a halt to WLFI's application for a national trust banking license unless the Trump family sells its stake in WLFI; the OCC has rejected Warren's request. In a letter to Warren, OCC Comptroller General Jonathan Gould stated, "The OCC intends to fulfill its review responsibilities, not to satisfy your request." Crypto companies have long faced difficulties obtaining national trust banking licenses in the United States. However, a breakthrough was achieved last December when the OCC conditionally approved five digital asset institutions—Ripple, Circle, Paxos, BitGo, and Fidelity Digital Assets—to become federally chartered national trust banks. ZachXBT, in an article on the X platform, stated, "If you're wondering how John Daghita (Lick) stole over $40 million from a U.S. government seizure address, here's what's currently known: John's father owns CMDSS, a company that currently holds an ongoing U.S. government IT contract in Virginia. CMDSS has a contract to assist the U.S. Marshals Service (USMS) in managing and disposing of seized/confiscated crypto assets. As of now, it remains unclear how John obtained this access through his father." Important Economic Developments

▌Sanae Takaichi's Tough Stance Causes a Squeeze on Yen Shorts

With Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi issuing a warning about abnormal exchange rate fluctuations, traders will be on high alert at the start of this week, wary of Japanese government intervention to stem the recent yen's decline—an action that may even receive rare assistance from the United States. Michael Brown, senior research strategist at Pepperstone Group, said: "Exchange rate checks are usually the last warning before intervention. The Takaichi government seems to have a much lower tolerance for foreign exchange speculation than its predecessors." Reports of exchange rate checks may deter the market from further shorting the yen, thus squeezing yen short positions, which have reached their largest increase in over a decade. In the last few trading hours of last week, the yen fluctuated wildly, reversing its downward trend toward 2024 lows, surging as much as 1.75% against the dollar to 155.63, its largest single-day gain since August last year.

Nick, chief analyst at AT Global Markets, pointed out that if the US participates in potential currency checks, the impact will not be limited to the yen but could also affect global markets. According to Solid Intel, the dollar's share of global foreign exchange reserves has fallen to its lowest level this century. The yen rose about 0.8% in early trading on Monday, driving up non-US currencies. The dollar/yen pair fell about 0.78% to 154.49 in early trading on Monday after Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi vowed to take action on currency volatility. The dollar/CHF pair fell more than 0.6% to 0.7751, while the euro/dollar pair rose 0.4% to 1.1873. Spot gold has historically broken through the $5,000 mark, just over 100 days after first breaking the $4,000 mark (October 8, 2025). Market analysis points out that macroeconomic factors such as increased gold purchases by central banks, geopolitical tensions, and economic uncertainty are the reasons for the recent rise in gold prices. The probability of the Federal Reserve keeping interest rates unchanged in January is 97.2%. According to CME's "FedWatch," the probability of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates by 25 basis points in January is 2.8%, while the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 97.2%. The probability of a cumulative 25 basis point rate cut by March is 15.5%, the probability of maintaining the current rate is 84.1%, and the probability of a cumulative 50 basis point rate cut is 0.4%. Artificial intelligence (AI) is gradually being integrated into cryptocurrency trading, accelerating the analysis, execution, and optimization processes previously performed manually. Investors and trading firms are facing the challenge of automating decision-making without compromising control, accountability, or human judgment. While some projects are striving to build more autonomous trading systems, most AI tools in the cryptocurrency space remain limited. Humans are still responsible for strategy development, setting risk limits, and bearing responsibility for outcomes, while machines handle most of the bandwidth used for data-intensive tasks such as research and monitoring. In the cryptocurrency market, the balance between automation and regulation is quietly reshaping trading workflows and beginning to redefine which human roles remain important. Artificial intelligence hasn't eliminated humans from the cryptocurrency space, but it's reshaping how labor is distributed across the entire industry. This shift is largely happening quietly, at the task level, particularly in research roles that previously relied on teams of junior analysts and interns. As trade execution becomes increasingly automated, traders can focus their energy on strategy and risk control rather than manual operations.

data.title: "Sister Wood's" Major Annual Project: ARK 2026 Big Idea
data.description:
If you follow global technology investment, it's almost impossible to ignore one name—Cathie Wood, better known as "Sister Wood" by Chinese investors. For the past decade, she and her firm ARK Invest have been doing something that doesn't always work on Wall Street: ignoring short-term noise and betting on long-term, extreme, and non-linear technological changes. ARK's annual research report, *Big Ideas*, has been published for ten consecutive years. It's not just a simple industry outlook; it's more like a "technology map for the next ten years." You may disagree with its conclusions, but it's hard to ignore the questions it raises. And this year's *ARK Big Ideas 2026* has a very striking overarching title: The Great Acceleration. This report focuses on 13 major innovation areas, with its core conclusion being that five major innovation platforms centered on artificial intelligence are rapidly converging, which will trigger a leapfrog change in global economic growth by the end of this decade, with the real GDP growth rate expected to reach 7.3% in 2030, 4 percentage points higher than the IMF's forecast of 3.1%. The report's most important assessment is that AI is not just another major technological advancement, but rather a "central dynamite" simultaneously driving the acceleration of multiple technology curves. For decades, technological innovation has largely followed a linear structure: one technology → one industry → one capital cycle. ARK argues that this paradigm has become obsolete. At the current stage, technologies are no longer parallel but highly coupled and mutually unlocking: AI's computing power demands are driving the next generation of cloud, energy storage, and data center revolutions; blockchain and digital wallets provide a trusted settlement and execution layer for AI agents; robotics and autonomous driving are propelling AI from the "digital world" into the "physical world"; and multi-omics and programmable biology provide AI with high-dimensional life data, in turn accelerating model capabilities. ARK uses a metric to describe this state: Convergence Network Strength. By 2025, this metric had increased by 35% year-on-year—meaning that the mutual catalysis between different technologies is accelerating significantly. This is why ARK calls 2026: The Great Acceleration. ARK research shows that reusable rockets launching AI chips into orbit, multi-omics data driving the development of precision therapies, and smart contracts supporting AI agents coordinating real-world resources—these seemingly independent innovations are creating unprecedented synergies. The importance of robotics as a catalyst will reach an inflection point in 2025, and energy storage and distributed energy systems have become key drivers for the construction of next-generation cloud infrastructure. The report states that the direct impacts of this technological revolution are: The market share of innovative assets will grow from approximately 20% in 2025 to approximately 50% in 2030, with their market capitalization potentially expanding from the current approximately $5 trillion to around $28 trillion. Investment in data center systems is projected to grow from approximately $500 billion in 2025 to approximately $1.4 trillion in 2030, representing a compound annual growth rate of 30%. Commercialization in areas such as autonomous taxis, AI drug development, and home humanoid robots is accelerating, with some areas already entering the stage of large-scale deployment. However, ARK also clearly points out that not all compelling technologies are disruptive. The report uses quantum computing as an example, arguing that even at the most aggressive rate of development, its practical application in cryptographic decryption won't be realized until the 2040s. Truly disruptive technologies must meet conditions such as drastically reduced costs, compelling unit economics across multiple industries, and the ability to serve as a platform for other technological innovations.

AI Leads the Era of "The Great Acceleration"

The report states that ARK has named this round of technological change "The Great Acceleration," arguing that the interdependence of five major innovation platforms—AI, public blockchain, robotics, energy storage, and multi-omics—is increasing, with performance improvements in one platform unlocking new capabilities in another.

The most striking example in the report is the combination of reusable rockets and AI computing power. The demand for next-generation cloud computing capabilities from neural networks is facing limitations in ground-based expansion, and reusable rockets may offer a solution.

At a competitive cost, space-based AI computing power could provide the cloud with computing capabilities unrestricted by ground-based power and cooling. ARK's analysis shows that the growth in AI chips could increase the demand for reusable rockets by approximately 60 times relative to existing models. At anticipated launch costs, space-based computing could be 25% cheaper than ground-based computing. This technological convergence is reportedly fueling an unprecedented investment cycle. ARK research indicates that capital investment alone could contribute 1.9 percentage points to annualized real GDP growth this decade. New capital bases—autonomous taxis, next-generation data centers, and corporate investments in AI agents—should boost return on invested capital. As other innovations begin to impact growth trajectories, actual growth could exceed consensus expectations by more than 4 percentage points annually. Historically, technological paradigm shifts have repeatedly triggered structural changes in GDP growth rates. ARK data shows that global real GDP growth has gradually increased from 0.037% in 100,000 BC, through stages such as the Agricultural Revolution and the Industrial Revolution, to around 3% currently. This current technological revolution, centered on AI, could push this growth rate above 7%.

AI Infrastructure Investment Surges

Investment in data center systems is growing at an accelerating rate. Since the launch of ChatGPT, the annualized growth rate of such investments has jumped from 5% to 29%.

Robots: A Severely Underestimated GDP Engine

If AI is the main engine of the digital world, then robots are its most important "physical export."

The report emphasizes that the rapid advancement of AI is transforming robots from specialized equipment for fixed tasks into relatively open, general-purpose platforms, which is key to unlocking the potential of industrial and domestic markets.

ARK estimates that the global robotics market has a revenue opportunity of approximately $26 trillion, divided into two main segments: manufacturing and domestic services.

ARK estimates that the global robotics market has a revenue opportunity of approximately $26 trillion, divided into two main segments: manufacturing and domestic services.

In the manufacturing sector, global manufacturing GDP is projected to reach $32 trillion by 2030. If robotics technology can achieve a 100% increase in labor productivity, and based on a 35% service provider revenue share, it could create approximately $13 trillion in income opportunities. In the domestic services sector, approximately 2.8 billion people worldwide perform 2.3 hours of unpaid domestic work daily. Based on a global average hourly wage of $12 and a 50% time value, this also corresponds to a market space of approximately $13 trillion. ARK particularly emphasized the macro-level significance of humanoid robots. A fact that is easily overlooked is that today, a significant amount of household maintenance, care, cleaning, and management labor is not included in GDP. ARK's calculations show that a single household humanoid robot could convert approximately $62,000 of implicit labor into explicit GDP annually; if 80% of US households adopt it within 5 years, the annual GDP growth rate could jump from 2-3% to 5-6%. The report argues that this is not a story of "job replacement," but rather about transforming non-market activities into market activities, freeing up time for productivity. Autonomous driving is reaching a turning point. ARK estimates that the complexity of humanoid robots is approximately 200,000 times higher than that of self-driving cars. This complexity ratio defines the theoretical capabilities required to achieve full autonomy. Nevertheless, by mapping the relationship between the computational demands and performance improvements required for Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD), ARK predicts that, with continued expansion of AI computing power and hardware advancements, the Optimus humanoid robot may reach human-level task execution capabilities around 2028. Autonomous taxis are beginning to erode the market share of ride-hailing services. In the San Francisco operating area, Waymo's market share has already put pressure on Uber and Lyft. Companies like Waymo, Baidu's Apollo Go, and Pony.ai have accumulated billions of miles of autonomous driving mileage, with daily driverless mileage growing rapidly. Decreasing costs will be key to driving demand. ARK predicts that by 2035, the price per mile for autonomous taxis globally could drop to $0.25, far lower than the $2.80 for human-driven ride-hailing services and $0.80 for private cars in the US in 2025. In the early stages of commercialization, vehicle costs will dominate unit economics, while increased vehicle utilization at scale will drive down the cost per mile. The market potential is enormous. ARK estimates that by 2030, autonomous taxis could generate approximately $34 trillion in enterprise value, with autonomous driving technology providers capturing about 98% of the EBIT (earnings before interest and taxes) and enterprise value, while automakers and fleet operators will have a relatively smaller share. The main risk to this forecast lies in whether automakers other than Tesla can scale their autonomous taxi fleets quickly enough.

Autonomous logistics also holds great promise. Fully automated last-mile delivery—whether via drones or ground robots—has already exceeded 4 million trips annually worldwide. Driverless long-haul trucking has launched in the US, and operators are planning rapid route expansion. ARK predicts that global revenue from automated delivery could reach $480 billion by 2030, with automation of oversight and back-end loading operations being significant limiting factors.

...

Multiomics and AI-Driven Breakthroughs in Biology

The combination of multiomics—encompassing genomics, epigenomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics—with AI is creating a flywheel effect for biological innovation. This flywheel includes: generating richer, lower-cost biological data, conducting more accurate tests, generating better biological insights, developing AI-driven drugs, and ultimately curing diseases.

The cost of data generation is decreasing dramatically.

The cost of whole-genome sequencing may drop to $10 by 2030, about 10 times lower than in 2015. This will drive a surge in sequencing demand, with the number of next-generation molecular diagnostic tests projected to grow from less than one million in 2020 to approximately 7 million in 2030. The amount of token data generated annually could reach approximately 200 billion, exceeding the 150 trillion tokens used to train cutting-edge language models such as OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and xAI. AI-powered diagnostic capabilities are reaching a turning point. Following the launch of ChatGPT, the success rate of FDA-approved AI-driven tests and devices has shifted from single-digit percentages. ARK's best-fit model shows that the proportion of AI-driven diagnostics and devices may expand to approximately 30% by 2030, eventually reaching close to 100%. Drug development economics is being reshaped. AI-driven drug development could shorten time to market by approximately 40%, from 13 years to 8 years, while reducing total drug costs by approximately four times, from $2.4 billion to $700 million. Combining the acceleration of AI and the potential for disease cures, AI-designed drugs in Phase I clinical trials could be worth over $2 billion, while traditional drug assets typically only recoup their capital costs. The market potential for biocure is particularly staggering. ARK research shows that the average price of curing a rare disease currently exceeds $1 million, nearly 15 times the cost of a lifetime prescription required to manage the disease. Cure drugs can generate revenue from a large portion of the patient population before patent expiration, potentially being 20 times more valuable than typical drugs and 2.4 times more valuable than prescription drugs for chronic diseases. A broader perspective is the extension of healthy lifespan. If the US population could live to the theoretical maximum lifespan of 120 years under perfect health conditions, but the risk of accidental death still exists, this would result in a gain of 11.9 billion mass-adjusted life years (QALYs). At an estimated $100,000 per healthy life year, the potential market opportunity for this lifespan gain is approximately $1.2 trillion. The current global biotechnology market represents only about 0.1% of this potential market. Reusable rockets are ushering in the space economy. SpaceX's reusable rocket technology is propelling the economy into the space age. By 2025, the mass of satellites launched into orbit annually will reach a record high, with SpaceX dominating the market. The company owns over 9,000 active Starlink satellites, approximately 66% of all active satellites in Earth orbit. Launch costs continue to decline. According to Wright's Law, launch costs should decrease by approximately 17% for every doubling of cumulative launch mass. Over the 17 years since 2008, leveraging the partial reusability of the Falcon 9, SpaceX has reduced costs by approximately 95%, from about $15,600 per kilogram to less than $1,000. ARK research suggests that Starship could extend this trajectory to below $100 per kilogram at scale. Satellite bandwidth costs are also declining. According to Reiter's Law, satellite bandwidth costs should decrease by approximately 44% for every doubling of cumulative orbital gigabits per second (Gbps), enabling satellite connections to supplement cellular towers and provide ubiquitous mobile coverage throughout the United States. The comparison shows that in 2001, the monthly fee for a mobile connection for US consumers was approximately $90 (in 2025 dollars), including only 0.001GB of data and covering about 1% of the US land area; in 2025, the monthly fee will be approximately $100, providing unlimited high-speed internet and covering about 86% of the land area; by 2030, it is projected to achieve 100% coverage at the same price. The market opportunity is substantial. Thanks to cost reductions and performance improvements, large-scale satellite connectivity could generate over $160 billion in revenue annually, representing approximately 15% of ARK's global communications revenue forecast. This prediction, based on the relationship between constellation bandwidth capacity and revenue opportunities, shows exponential growth potential. Distributed energy supports AI computing power needs. Energy is increasingly driving economic growth. Despite concerns about energy intensity during the internet boom, economies have actually become more energy-efficient, and the AI ​​era may see a repeat of this dynamic. The energy intensity (kilowatt-hours required per dollar of GDP) of major economies such as China, the United States, Japan, India, and Germany has been declining steadily over the past three decades. The cost of multi-omics data has plummeted. Solar and battery costs continue to decline following Reiter's Law. Nuclear cost declines were interrupted in the 1970s due to regulatory changes, but recent US executive orders should push nuclear energy back onto its previous cost-declining trajectory. Historically, solar and nuclear costs (per megawatt-hour) and battery costs (per megawatt-hour) have decreased significantly with each doubling of cumulative capacity. Electricity prices are expected to resume their downward trend. According to Reiter's Law, ARK research shows that, except during World War II, US electricity prices steadily declined from the late 19th century to 1974, before being interrupted by increased regulatory costs leading to higher nuclear power plant construction. If regulations had not been tightened, ARK research suggests that today's electricity prices might be about 40% lower than they actually are. As low-cost power generation scales up and serves power-intensive AI data centers, retail electricity prices should begin to decline again after a 50-year stagnation. The investment demand is enormous. Given ARK's rapid GDP growth forecast, cumulative capital expenditure in the global power generation sector must increase by approximately two times to approximately $10 trillion by 2030 to meet global electricity demand. Therefore, fixed energy storage deployment needs to expand by approximately 19 times. Data centers are projected to account for approximately 5% of total power generation investment between 2026 and 2030.

The digital asset market is showing an evolving trend

Influenced by the regulatory framework that may be brought about by the GENIUS Act, stablecoin activity saw significant growth in 2025. Several companies and institutions announced stablecoin-related plans, and BlackRock disclosed that it was preparing an internal tokenization platform. Stablecoin issuers and fintech companies such as Tether, Circle, and Stripe launched or supported Layer 1 blockchains optimized for stablecoins.
